title: What I learned building a churn prediction model
summary: Notes from my first end-to-end classification project — what went wrong, what I'd do differently, and the one feature that mattered most.
body:
Going in, I expected the modeling step to be the hard part. It wasn't — the hard part was deciding what counted as a 'feature' versus noise in a dataset with a lot of correlated columns.

A few takeaways:

1. Tenure and contract type carried most of the predictive signal. Adding more features past that point gave diminishing returns.
2. Recall mattered more than raw accuracy here, since missing a churner is costlier than a false alarm.
3. Cross-validation caught an overfitting issue that a single train/test split would have hidden completely.

Next time I'd spend more of my time budget on feature engineering and less on trying exotic models.
